digital_id.note: The National Identification Number (NIN), issued and centrally held by the National Identity Management Commission (NIMC, a federal agency), is a mandatory prerequisite for opening bank accounts, registering SIM cards, obtaining a passport, voter registration, tax payment, pension/insurance claims and access to government services. The 2026 NIMC Act overhauled the two-decade-old legal framework, and a new General Multipurpose Identity Card (GMPC) began rolling out in 2025. It is a centralized, state-held database, not a citizen-controlled system; the president has directed NIMC to enroll every Nigerian, though full nationwide enrollment is still in progress.

sources.title: Nigeria Makes National ID Number Mandatory for Access to Social Services - ID Tech
Nigeria pushes for universal digital ID as NIN use cases expand

cash_payment.note: Cash (naira) is legal tender and remains widely used, especially outside major cities, and the Supreme Court ordered in November 2023 that old naira notes remain legal tender indefinitely after the CBN's 2022-2023 naira redesign caused a severe cash-scarcity crisis. However, the CBN's 'Payments Vision 2025' explicitly aims to push the country toward a cashless, electronic-payments-dominant system, and its cashless policy imposes withdrawal limits that constrain cash access, creating real friction alongside a fast-growing e-payments sector.

sources.title: Why Nigeria's Controversial Naira Redesign Policy Hasn't Met Its Objectives - Carnegie Endowment
New naira: Nigeria to become cashless by 2025 - CBN

mandatory_schooling.note: Basic education is compulsory and nominally free under Section 18(3)(a) of the Constitution and the Compulsory, Free Universal Basic Education Act 2004 (covering pre-primary through junior secondary, to roughly age 15). Homeschooling is not explicitly banned, but also has no clear legal status; a bill to formally legalize it as an optional alternative passed first reading in the House of Representatives in late 2024 and remained pending. The real story is the gap between law and practice: UNICEF's 2024 data puts Nigeria's out-of-school children at approximately 18.3 million (10.2 million primary-age, 8.1 million secondary-age) despite the compulsory, free legal mandate.

sources.title: Reps consider bill to legalise homeschooling

social_credit.note: Nigeria has no government-operated social or behavioral credit/trust scoring system for citizens. What exists is a conventional private-sector financial credit scoring market: three licensed credit bureaus (CRC Credit Bureau, FirstCentral Credit Bureau, CreditRegistry) produce FICO-style creditworthiness scores (300-850) based purely on borrowing/repayment history, used by lenders for loan decisions — not a state tool for rating overall citizen trustworthiness or restricting non-financial rights.

alt_currency.note: Cryptocurrency was effectively restricted after a 2021 CBN directive barring banks from servicing crypto exchanges, but the Investments and Securities Act 2025 (signed by President Tinubu, 29 March 2025) formally recognized digital assets as securities under SEC oversight: individuals do not need a license to buy/hold crypto, but exchanges and other Virtual Asset Service Providers must register with the SEC, and from 2026 crypto profits are taxable up to 25%. No broad restriction on private gold ownership or trading was found in available sources.

sources.title: Nigeria's president signs bill recognizing digital assets into law
Can I Now Legally Invest in Crypto from Nigeria? Here's the SEC's Stance

mandatory_insurance.note: Two specific mandatory insurance regimes exist rather than a broad universal requirement. The National Health Insurance Act 2022 (Sections 3 and 14) legally mandates health insurance for all Nigerians and legal residents, and in 2024 President Tinubu directed all federal ministries/agencies to enroll staff; actual coverage remains low (about 21.7 million people, roughly 13% of the population, as of 2025). Separately, motor third-party liability insurance is legally mandatory and has been under active nationwide police/NAICOM enforcement since 1 February 2025.

sources.title: Presidential Directive on Mandatory Health Insurance Implementation Across MDAs - NHIA
Explainer: What to know about third-party vehicle insurance, police enforcement in Nigeria

travel_restrictions.note: There is no general nationwide restriction on citizen movement, and police checkpoints are a routine (not emergency) feature of daily travel. However, federal/state/local governments have imposed curfews and movement restrictions in specific conflict-affected states (Borno, Yobe, Adamawa) in connection with operations against Boko Haram/ISIS-West Africa, and several states have enacted anti-open-grazing laws that restrict the traditional movement of Fulani herders, contributing to escalating farmer-herder conflict.

sources.title: Nigeria - 2021 Country Reports on Human Rights Practices - US State Department
How to stop Nigeria's worsening farmer-pastoralist violence

internet_restrictions.note: There is no permanent nationwide internet block, but the government banned Twitter/X entirely for seven months (June 2021-January 2022) after the platform removed a presidential post, reinstating it only after Twitter agreed to local registration, a country representative, and tax payment. Freedom House's Freedom on the Net 2025 report documents continued targeted online repression — e.g., social-media commentator Isaac Bristol was detained roughly three weeks and charged under the Cybercrimes Act for a hashtag campaign — plus over 30 network disruptions in May 2025 alone (mostly infrastructure-related, not deliberate state shutdowns), and one newly registered internet-restriction case in 2025.

sources.title: Nigeria: Freedom on the Net 2025 Country Report | Freedom House
Nigeria's Twitter Ban Is a Bellwether Case for Internet Freedom

free_speech.note: Freedom of speech and the press are constitutionally guaranteed but significantly limited in practice by sedition, criminal defamation and 'false news' laws, plus the Cybercrimes Act (amended February 2024 to narrow but not eliminate its use against 'cyberstalking'/false-information speech). Reporters Without Borders ranked Nigeria 123rd of 180 countries in the 2025 World Press Freedom Index, down from prior years. Human Rights Watch's 2025 World Report documents authorities responding to criticism of economic hardship under President Tinubu with arrests and prosecutions of journalists, social-media commentators and protesters; at least 56 journalists were reportedly assaulted or arrested while covering protests in August 2024 alone.

sources.title: World Report 2025: Nigeria | Human Rights Watch
